metadata.title: Prolonged fasting-induced metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle of lean and obese men
metadata.study_design: Experimental study comparing lean and obese participants during fasting periods
metadata.demographics: 18 apparently healthy young men aged from 20 to 35 years; nine lean (BMI 19–23) and nine obese (BMI 32–40 kg/m²)

evidence.quote: Prolonged fasting-induced metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle of lean and obese men
18 apparently healthy young men aged from 20 to 35 years participated; nine lean (BMI 19–23) and nine obese (BMI 32–40 kg/m²)
we examined nine obese and nine lean participants during 12 and 72 h of fasting, respectively
Plasma glucose was measured immediately on an YSI 2300 STAT Plus glucose analyzer (YSI 1)
All participants had low levels of physical activity, were non-smokers and used no prescription medications
Glucagon was analyzed by RIA (EMD Millipore, Germany)
Commercial kits were used to analyze FFA (Wako Chemicals, Germany), glycerol and β-hydroxybutyrate (Cayman Chemicals, USA)

methods_participants.label: Metabolomic Analysis Method
methods_participants.value: Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry

narrative_results.statement: HbA1c was within the normal range for all participants, although mean HbA1c in mmol/mol was ~10% higher in obese vs. lean participants

Insulin-stimulation suppressed FFA levels significantly in both groups, but less so during prolonged fasting, resulting in ~3–4 fold higher plasma FFA levels during the HEC during 72h of fasting compared to 12h of fasting
Endogenous glucose production (EGP), consisting of hepatic glycogenolysis and both hepatic and renal gluconeogenesis were reduced during prolonged fasting
Insulin stimulation during 72h of fasting decreased glucose oxidation and GS phosphorylation indicating that glucose incorporation into glycogen was prioritized
The capacity for glycogenesis in the obese, measured as insulin-stimulated NOGD during 12h fast, was reduced by ~45%, but this did not translate into lower glycogen content

The metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle during acute and chronic insulin resistance reveal distinct differences. Impaired insulin signaling in skeletal muscle may subserve insulin resistance in obesity, whereas insulin resistance associated with prolonged fasting

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 describes Control fasting (12 hours) while Pass 2 describes Intervention fasting (72 hours). These are different experimental conditions, not the same fact.
Pass 1 is about Fasting Duration - Intervention (72 hours) while Pass 2 is about Hyperinsulinemic Euglycemic Clamp Duration (2 hours). These are completely different measurements.
Pass 1 describes HEC Duration (2 hours) while Pass 2 describes HEC Insulin Infusion Rate (30 mU/m2/min). These are different parameters of the same procedure.

Pass_1 discusses non-insulin-stimulated plasma FFA concentrations in obese vs lean during fasting. Pass_2 discusses glucose production in non-insulin-stimulated state. These are different metabolic measures and cannot both be correct for the same aligned pair.

Pass_1 discusses leptin level changes during prolonged fasting in obese vs lean. Pass_2 discusses metabolic adaptation and glycogen breakdown in insulin resistant obese muscle. These are fundamentally different findings.
Pass 1 states prolonged fasting increased triglyceride accumulation in skeletal muscle. Pass 2 states glycogen levels were reduced ~20%. These are different metabolites and different findings.
Pass 1 discusses β-OHB metabolism in fasting muscle. Pass 2 discusses TCA-cycle intermediates in obese vs lean participants. These are completely different findings.

Pass 1 discusses TCA-cycle intermediates (citrate, succinate, malate) reduced in obese vs lean. Pass 2 discusses acetylcarnitine levels during different fasting durations. These are different metabolites and different comparisons.
Pass 1 discusses acetylcarnitine levels during fasting in obese vs lean. Pass 2 discusses GS phosphorylation during prolonged fasting. These are different metabolic markers.

Pass 1 discusses glucose production differences between obese and lean during 12h fasting. Pass 2 discusses glycogenesis capacity and glycogen content in obese during 12h fast. These are different metabolic processes and comparisons.

Pass_1 describes glycogen levels reduced ~20% during prolonged fasting in skeletal muscle. Pass_2 is a general statement about metabolic signatures in insulin resistance with no specific quantitative data. These describe different facts.

Pass 1 discusses HbA1c levels and their comparison between obese and lean participants. Pass 2 discusses leptin levels during fasting. These are entirely different biomarkers and findings from different experimental contexts.

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: we examined nine obese and nine lean participants during 12 and 72 h of fasting, respectively

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Randomized crossover design with two study occasions separated by minimum 21 days

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: we examined nine obese and nine lean participants during 12 and 72 h of fasting, respectively
18 apparently healthy young men aged from 20 to 35 years participated
nine lean (BMI 19–23) and nine obese (BMI 32–40 kg/m2)
nine lean (BMI 19–23) and nine obese (BMI 32–40 kg/m2)
All participants had low levels of physical activity, were non-smokers and used no prescription medications
All participants had low levels of physical activity, were non-smokers and used no prescription medications
All participants had low levels of physical activity, were non-smokers and used no prescription medications
participants were examined on two occasions separated by a minimum of 21 days: 1) in the post absorptive state during an overnight fast of 12 h (control)
During a constant infusion of insulin (Actrapid, NovoNordisk, Denmark) at the rate of 30 mU insulin/m2/min, plasma glucose levels were clamped at ~5 mM
Hyperinsulinemic Euglycemic Clamp - Insulin Infusion Rate
followed by a two h hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp (HEC) (t=240–360)
a muscle specimen of approximately 100 mg was obtained from vastus lateralis of the quadriceps femoris muscle using a modified Bergström method
At t=60 and 270 min., under sterile conditions and using local anesthesia, a muscle specimen of approximately 100 mg was obtained
At t=60 and 270 min., under sterile conditions and using local anesthesia, a muscle specimen of approximately 100 mg was obtained
Body composition was determined by dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry scan (QDR-2000; Hologic, USA)
Plasma glucose was measured immediately on an YSI 2300 STAT Plus glucose analyzer (YSI 1)
Insulin, C-peptide, and cortisol were measured by ELISA (DAKO, Agilent Technologies, Denmark (insulin)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Vastus lateralis of quadriceps femoris muscle

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Insulin, C-peptide, and cortisol were measured by ELISA (DAKO, Agilent Technologies, Denmark (insulin), ALPCO, NH, USA (C-peptide)
Insulin, C-peptide, and cortisol were measured by ELISA (DAKO, Agilent Technologies, Denmark (insulin), ALPCO, NH, USA (C-peptide) and DRG, NJ, USA (cortisol))
Glucagon was analyzed by RIA (EMD Millipore, Germany)
Commercial kits were used to analyze FFA (Wako Chemicals, Germany)
Commercial kits were used to analyze FFA (Wako Chemicals, Germany), glycerol and β-hydroxybutyrate (Cayman Chemicals, USA)
Commercial kits were used to analyze FFA (Wako Chemicals, Germany), glycerol and β-hydroxybutyrate (Cayman Chemicals, USA)

The respiratory exchange ratio (RER) and resting energy expenditure (EE) were measured by indirect calorimetry (Deltatrac monitor, Dantes Instrumentarium, Finland) performed at t=180–210 and t=300–330

The respiratory exchange ratio (RER) and resting energy expenditure (EE) were measured by indirect calorimetry (Deltatrac monitor, Dantes Instrumentarium, Finland) performed at t=180–210 and t=300–330
In a randomized crossover design, participants were examined on two occasions separated by a minimum of 21 days
participants were examined on two occasions separated by a minimum of 21 days

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: HbA1c was within the normal range for all participants, although mean HbA1c in mmol/mol was ~10% higher in obese vs. lean participants
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Non-insulin-stimulated insulin levels were ~2–3 fold higher in obese than lean and decreased by at least 50% during 72h of fasting in both groups

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Non-insulin-stimulated insulin levels were ~2–3 fold higher in obese than lean and decreased by at least 50% during 72h of fasting in both groups
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: During the HEC, insulin levels were ~15–30% higher in obese than lean

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: During the HEC, insulin levels were ~15–30% higher in obese than lean
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Prolonged fasting prompted a greater decrease in non-insulin-stimulated plasma glucose levels in lean (~35%) than obese (~25%)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Prolonged fasting prompted a greater decrease in non-insulin-stimulated plasma glucose levels in lean (~35%) than obese (~25%), despite higher insulin levels in obese
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Insulin-stimulation suppressed FFA levels significantly in both groups, but less so during prolonged fasting, resulting in ~3–4 fold higher plasma FFA levels during the HEC during 72h of fasting compared to 12h of fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Insulin-stimulation suppressed FFA levels significantly in both groups, but less so during prolonged fasting, resulting in ~3–4 fold higher plasma FFA levels during the HEC during 72h of fasting compared to 12h of fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: We did not find increased FFA content in skeletal muscle from obese participants at any time point despite previous observations of a higher lipolytic rate during 12h of fasting in obese participants

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Non-insulin-stimulated plasma FFA concentrations were higher in obese during an overnight fast, but lower during prolonged fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The increased fat mass in obese was associated with higher levels of leptin and lower levels of adiponectin which is in agreement with the observed insulin resistance

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Despite previous observations of a higher lipolytic rate during 12h of fasting in obese participants, increased FFA content in skeletal muscle from obese participants was not found at any time point
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Prolonged fasting reduced circulating leptin levels although at a higher level in the obese participants

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: The increased fat mass in obese was associated with higher levels of leptin and lower levels of adiponectin which is in agreement with the observed insulin resistance
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Despite an abundant supply of long-chain fatty acids from plasma during prolonged fasting, we found only modest increases in long chain fatty acids and no increases in acylcarnitines in skeletal muscle

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Prolonged fasting reduced circulating leptin levels although at a higher level in the obese participants
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: There were no changes in NAD+, NADH, NADP+, FAD and AMP, ADP, ATP associated with prolonged fasting, indicating myocellular energetics were preserved

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Despite an abundant supply of long-chain fatty acids from plasma during prolonged fasting, only modest increases in long chain fatty acids and no increases in acylcarnitines in skeletal muscle were found
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: In obese participants, the levels of the TCA-cycle intermediates citrate, succinate, and malate were reduced compared to lean independently of the duration of the fast

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Prolonged fasting increased triglyceride accumulation in skeletal muscle
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Acetylcarnitine in obese were lower during 12h of fasting, but normalized to levels similar to lean during 72h of fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Very high levels of β-OHB in the fasting muscle and a concomitant increase in β-OHB-carnitine were found, demonstrating that ketone bodies were metabolized in skeletal muscle
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Endogenous glucose production, consisting of hepatic glycogenolysis and both hepatic and renal gluconeogenesis were reduced during prolonged fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: There were no changes in NAD+, NADH, NADP+, FAD and AMP, ADP, ATP associated with prolonged fasting, indicating that myocellular energetics were preserved and there were no suggestions of deficient substrate oxidation and obvious mitochondrial defects despite insulin resistance
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Glucose production in the non-insulin-stimulated state was lower in obese than lean during 12h of fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: In obese participants, the levels of the TCA-cycle intermediates citrate, succinate, and malate were reduced compared to lean independently of the duration of the fast
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: In both obesity and fasting, insulin suppressed EGP indicating that, in contrast to muscle, hepatic sensitivity to insulin was preserved

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Acetylcarnitine in obese were lower during 12h of fasting, but normalized to levels similar to lean during 72h of fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The capacity for glycogenesis in the obese, measured as insulin-stimulated NOGD during 12h fast, was reduced by ~45%, but this did not translate into lower glycogen content

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Endogenous glucose production (EGP), consisting of hepatic glycogenolysis and both hepatic and renal gluconeogenesis were reduced during prolonged fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: During prolonged fasting, glycogen levels in skeletal muscle were reduced ~20% in both groups

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Glucose production in the non-insulin-stimulated state was lower in obese than lean during 12h of fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: GS phosphorylation increased during prolonged fasting also in obese, similar to lean participants

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: In both obesity and fasting, insulin suppressed EGP indicating that, in contrast to muscle, hepatic sensitivity to insulin was preserved
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Insulin stimulation during 72h of fasting decreased glucose oxidation and GS phosphorylation indicating that glucose incorporation into glycogen was prioritized

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: The capacity for glycogenesis in the obese, measured as insulin-stimulated NOGD during 12h fast, was reduced by ~45%, but this did not translate into lower glycogen content
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The metabolic adaptation to prolonged fasting involving changes in glycogen breakdown are lost in muscle from insulin resistant obese participants

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: During prolonged fasting, glycogen levels in skeletal muscle were reduced ~20% in both groups
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle during acute and chronic insulin resistance reveal distinct differences

semantic_groups.rationale: Both report 9 lean participants; pass_2 adds gender specification ('men') which is additional detail but same core fact
Both report 9 obese participants; pass_2 adds gender specification ('men') which is additional detail but same core fact
Identical age range criteria: 20 to 35 years
Identical BMI lean criteria: 19-23 kg/m2
Identical BMI obese criteria: 32-40 kg/m2
Identical physical activity criterion: low levels of physical activity
Identical smoking status criterion: non-smokers; minor label variation ('Smoking Status' vs 'Smoking')
Identical medication criterion: no prescription medications
Pass 1 describes Control fasting (12 hours) while Pass 2 describes Intervention fasting (72 hours). These are different experimental conditions, not the same fact.
Pass 1 is about Fasting Duration - Intervention (72 hours) while Pass 2 is about Hyperinsulinemic Euglycemic Clamp Duration (2 hours). These are completely different measurements.
Pass 1 describes HEC Duration (2 hours) while Pass 2 describes HEC Insulin Infusion Rate (30 mU/m2/min). These are different parameters of the same procedure.
Pass 1 contains Insulin Infusion Rate data but Pass 2 is empty. This is an orphan entry from Pass 1.
Pass 1 contains Glucose Target Level data but Pass 2 is empty. This is an orphan entry from Pass 1.
Both describe Muscle Biopsy Timing 1 at 60 minutes using Bergström method variants. Minor wording differences (Bergström vs modified Bergström) and unit abbreviation (minutes vs min) are negligible.
Both describe Muscle Biopsy Timing 2 at 270 minutes using Bergström method variants. Minor wording differences and unit abbreviation are negligible.
Both describe Muscle Biopsy Sample Size of approximately 100 mg using Bergström method variants. Minor wording differences (Bergström vs modified Bergström) are negligible.
Pass 1 contains muscle biopsy location information; Pass 2 is empty
Both describe DEXA scan for body composition; Pass 2 adds specific device model (QDR-2000) and manufacturer (Hologic, USA)
Both describe YSI 2300 STAT Plus glucose analyzer; Pass 1 labels it 'Glucose Measurement' while Pass 2 specifies 'Plasma Glucose Measurement'
Both describe ELISA for insulin assay; Pass 2 adds manufacturer details (DAKO, Agilent Technologies, Denmark)
Both describe ELISA for C-peptide assay; Pass 2 adds manufacturer details (ALPCO, NH, USA)
Both describe ELISA for cortisol assay; Pass 2 adds manufacturer details (DRG, NJ, USA)
Both describe RIA for glucagon assay; Pass 2 adds manufacturer details (EMD Millipore, Germany)
Pass 1 contains HbA1c assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains ALAT assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains TSH assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Both describe Indirect Calorimetry Timing 1 at 180-210 minutes; minor differences in formatting and additional equipment details in Pass 2 do not contradict the core fact
Both describe Indirect Calorimetry Timing 2 at 300-330 minutes; minor differences in formatting and additional equipment details in Pass 2 do not contradict the core fact
Both describe randomized crossover design; Pass 1 includes additional detail about 21-day separation while Pass 2 is more concise; same underlying fact
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains fasting duration information for control group
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains free fatty acids assay information
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains glycerol assay information
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains information about β-hydroxybutyrate assay kit from Cayman Chemicals. No comparison possible.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains information about a 21-day minimum washout period. No comparison possible.
Both extractions contain identical text describing non-insulin-stimulated insulin levels in obese vs lean subjects and fasting effects.
Both extractions contain identical text describing insulin levels during HEC in obese vs lean subjects.
Pass_1 includes additional clause 'despite higher insulin levels in obese' that Pass_2 omits. Same core fact about glucose level decreases, but Pass_1 provides more context.
Both extractions contain identical text describing FFA suppression and levels during fasting periods.

Pass_1 discusses non-insulin-stimulated plasma FFA concentrations in obese vs lean during fasting. Pass_2 discusses glucose production in non-insulin-stimulated state. These are different metabolic measures and cannot both be correct for the same aligned pair.
Both extractions convey the same finding: FFA content in skeletal muscle from obese participants was not increased despite higher lipolytic rates. Minor wording differences only.
Both extractions describe the same association between increased fat mass in obese and leptin/adiponectin levels related to insulin resistance. Negligible wording differences.

Pass_1 discusses leptin level changes during prolonged fasting in obese vs lean. Pass_2 discusses metabolic adaptation and glycogen breakdown in insulin resistant obese muscle. These are fundamentally different findings.

Both describe the same finding about modest increases in long-chain fatty acids and no increases in acylcarnitines during prolonged fasting. Minor wording differences ('were found' vs 'we found') are negligible.
Pass 1 states prolonged fasting increased triglyceride accumulation in skeletal muscle. Pass 2 states glycogen levels were reduced ~20%. These are different metabolites and different findings.
Pass 1 discusses β-OHB metabolism in fasting muscle. Pass 2 discusses TCA-cycle intermediates in obese vs lean participants. These are completely different findings.

Both describe no changes in NAD+, NADH, NADP+, FAD, and AMP, ADP, ATP during prolonged fasting indicating preserved myocellular energetics. Pass 1 adds interpretive details about substrate oxidation and mitochondrial function; Pass 2 is more concise but conveys the same core finding.

Pass 1 discusses TCA-cycle intermediates (citrate, succinate, malate) reduced in obese vs lean. Pass 2 discusses acetylcarnitine levels during different fasting durations. These are different metabolites and different comparisons.
Pass 1 discusses acetylcarnitine levels during fasting in obese vs lean. Pass 2 discusses GS phosphorylation during prolonged fasting. These are different metabolic markers.

Both describe the same finding that endogenous glucose production (consisting of hepatic glycogenolysis and hepatic/renal gluconeogenesis) was reduced during prolonged fasting. Wording is nearly identical.

Pass 1 discusses glucose production differences between obese and lean during 12h fasting. Pass 2 discusses glycogenesis capacity and glycogen content in obese during 12h fast. These are different metabolic processes and comparisons.

Both extractions contain identical core information about insulin suppressing EGP and hepatic sensitivity preservation. Pass_2 has minor trailing text '; finding' that appears to be a metadata artifact.
Pass_1 contains a complete statement about glycogenesis capacity reduction in obese individuals. Pass_2 is empty, making this an orphan extraction.

Pass_1 describes glycogen levels reduced ~20% during prolonged fasting in skeletal muscle. Pass_2 is a general statement about metabolic signatures in insulin resistance with no specific quantitative data. These describe different facts.
Pass_1 contains specific information about GS phosphorylation during prolonged fasting. Pass_2 is empty, making this an orphan extraction.

Both extractions contain identical core information about insulin stimulation during 72h fasting decreasing glucose oxidation and GS phosphorylation. Pass_2 has minor trailing text '; finding' that appears to be metadata.
Pass_1 contains detailed information about maltotriose reduction and metabolic adaptation to prolonged fasting. Pass_2 is empty, making this an orphan extraction.
Pass_1 contains a general statement about metabolic signatures in skeletal muscle. Pass_2 is empty, making this an orphan extraction.
Pass_1 contains information about impaired insulin signaling and ketone body metabolism in different conditions. Pass_2 is empty, making this an orphan extraction.

Pass 1 discusses HbA1c levels and their comparison between obese and lean participants. Pass 2 discusses leptin levels during fasting. These are entirely different biomarkers and findings from different experimental contexts.
